_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
procedure_name: Replacement Surgery for Corrosive Injury Stomach
source_files: Replacement Surgery for Corrosive Injury Stomach_Medco.pdf
Replacement Surgery for Corrosive Injury Stomach_PPD.CPD.pdf
clinical_indications: Stricture formation with an inability to swallow food after corrosive injury
Inability to swallow following a corrosive stricture of the oesophagus
Corrosive injury of the stomach (antrum, body, or pyloroduodenal area) leading to strictures
Corrosive injury of the oesophagus requiring a new passage (replacement)

preauth.label: Clinical Notes with history and examination and planned line of treatment

ppd_preauth.q: Were the patient's clinical history/investigations indicative of the Procedure?
Whether the investigation reports confirm the diagnosis?
Whether all the submitted reports are suggestive of history of upper GI corrosive injury?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details available?
Is the discharge summary with follow-up advice available at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Patient's clinical history or investigations not indicative of the procedure for corrosive injury stomach.
Investigation reports do not confirm the diagnosis of corrosive injury stomach.
Upper GI Endoscopy Report missing or incomplete at pre-authorization.
Barium Study missing or incomplete at pre-authorization.
CT Abdomen/Colonoscopy/Barium Enema missing or incomplete at pre-authorization.
Detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) with daily vitals and treatment details not submitted at claim stage.
Detailed Operative/Procedure Notes missing or incomplete at claim stage.
Detailed Discharge Summary with follow-up advice missing or incomplete at claim stage.

clinical_key_pointers: Potentially catastrophic presentation and lifelong complications resulting from caustic ingestion make it one of the most challenging clinical situations in gastroenterology.
Ingestion of caustic materials is accidental in children and usually suicidal in teenagers and adults.
Alkaline caustics and acids are the most common chemicals implicated in caustic burns.
Burns from such agents may include the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, oesophagus, and stomach.
Destruction of the tissues of these organs may lead to complications, of which respiratory compromise, oesophageal and gastric perforation, septicemia, or even death.
Acids commonly affect the stomach more than alkalis, cause mucosal damage by coagulation necrosis, and require a longer duration of contact.
Acids are cleared rapidly from the oesophagus to the stomach, pooling in the prepyloric area due to corrosive induced pylorospasm.
Strictures can also occur in the antrum, body, or the pyloroduodenal area.
Alkalis cause liquefaction necrosis, are more viscous, and tend to adhere to the oesophageal mucosa with only a relatively small amount reaching the stomach.
The extent of oesophageal damage is greater with alkalis than with acids.
Treatment options include dilations (blind, retrograde endless string, and endoscopic) or creating a new passage (replacement).
surgical_options: Bypass procedures (preferred over resection procedures due to reduced morbidity)
Gastric pull-up (technically easier, requires one anastomosis)
Colon graft (requires three anastomoses and extensive dissection)
